title: Topic 36: The Sunk Cost of Cheating: Why Banned Players Keep Coming Back
content:
# Topic 36: The Sunk Cost of Cheating: Why Banned Players Keep Coming Back

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the curious phenomenon of banned video game players who, despite being cast into the digital abyss for their transgressions, persistently claw their way back into the virtual worlds that rejected them. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this seemingly irrational behavior, from the magnetic pull of the sunk cost fallacy to the siren song of cognitive dissonance. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of real-world examples and established psychological theories, we posit that the banned player is not merely a digital pariah but a tragic hero of their own Sisyphean narrative. They are driven by a potent cocktail of investment, denial, and a misplaced sense of entitlement, forever doomed to repeat their cycle of virtual sin and redemption. This paper, therefore, is not just about cheating in video games; it is a profound exploration of the human condition, our inability to let go, and our unwavering belief that, this time, we definitely won't get caught.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand theater of online gaming, there exists a peculiar species of player: the un-bannable phoenix. Cast down from the hallowed servers for their digital malfeasance, they rise from the ashes of their deleted accounts, armed with a new email address and an unyielding sense of optimism. They are the ghosts in the machine, the persistent specters of servers past, and the living embodiment of the question: "Why can't you just let it go?" This paper, "The Sunk Cost of Cheating: Why Banned Players Keep Coming Back," is a scholarly, yet unapologetically comical, investigation into this very question.

We've all seen them. The player who, despite being banned for using an aimbot that could target a gnat from three virtual miles away, reappears a week later with a slightly altered username and the same uncanny accuracy. Or the one who, after being exiled for exploiting a glitch that allowed them to build a fortress of infinite gold, returns with a new character and a suspiciously large pile of gold. Are these individuals digital Houdinis, masters of evasion and disguise? Or are they simply victims of a powerful psychological force that compels them to return to the scene of the crime, like a moth to a flame, or a gamer to a 2-for-1 pizza deal?

This paper will argue for the latter. We will explore the psychological drivers behind this bizarre brand of loyalty, from the sunk cost fallacy that whispers sweet nothings about the thousands of hours and dollars invested, to the cognitive dissonance that allows a player to simultaneously believe they are a master of the game and that they need to cheat to win. We will journey through the annals of gaming history, from the early days of dial-up to the sprawling metaverses of today, to uncover the stories of the most infamous banned players and the psychological theories that explain their seemingly inexplicable behavior. So, grab your popcorn, silence your phones, and prepare to enter the mind of the banned gamer. It's a strange and wonderful place, full of contradictions, justifications, and a whole lot of cheat codes.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the matter of cheating in video games. While the specific phenomenon of the returning banned player has yet to receive its own dedicated magnum opus, a wealth of research on the psychology of cheating, addiction, and online behavior provides a solid, if slightly scattered, foundation for our investigation. Lee (2023) argues that the competitive nature of online games stimulates a user's motivation and psychological needs to a point where cheating becomes an attractive, if not necessary, option for some. This aligns with the work of Lee (2021), who suggests that cheating can be a manifestation of aggressive traits such as hostility and anger, which exist outside the context of the game itself. In other words, some people are just angry, and cheating at video games is how they choose to express it. A far healthier outlet than, say, competitive cheese rolling, but we digress.

Cognitive dissonance theory, as discussed by a particularly insightful board game enthusiast (2018), offers another compelling explanation. This theory posits that once a player makes the decision to cheat, subsequent cheating becomes more likely as they seek to reduce the mental discomfort of holding two contradictory beliefs: "I am a good player" and "I need to cheat to win." The cheater, therefore, is not just a rule-breaker, but a master of mental gymnastics, constantly contorting their own beliefs to justify their actions. This is further supported by research suggesting that self-esteem can be a significant factor in the decision to cheat (Anybrain, n.d.). For some, the validation of winning, even through illicit means, is a powerful balm for a fragile ego.

The concept of the sunk cost fallacy, while not exclusively a gaming-related theory, is perhaps the most crucial piece of the puzzle. As explained by Game Quitters (n.d.), the sunk cost fallacy is our tendency to continue an activity because of the time, money, or effort we've already invested, even when it no longer brings us joy. For the banned player, this fallacy is a siren's call, luring them back to the game with whispers of their past glories, their hard-earned loot, and the countless hours they've poured into their digital avatar. Phillips (2021) found that higher levels of investment in a game decrease the desire to quit, even when the game is no longer enjoyable. The banned player, therefore, is not just returning to a game; they are returning to an investment, a digital life that they have painstakingly built, and one that they are not willing to let go of, no matter how many times they are told to.

## ANALYSIS

The mind of the returning banned player is a fascinating and complex place, a swirling vortex of psychological phenomena that would make even Freud's head spin. To truly understand why these digital pariahs keep coming back for more, we must dissect the unholy trinity of their motivation: the sunk cost fallacy, cognitive dissonance, and the illusion of control.

The sunk cost fallacy, as we've established, is the primary antagonist in our story. The banned player has invested not just money, but time, effort, and a significant portion of their identity into the game. Their character is not just a collection of pixels; it is a digital extension of themselves, a carefully crafted persona with a rich history of triumphs and failures. To abandon this character is to abandon a part of themselves, a part that they have nurtured and developed over countless hours of gameplay. The ban, therefore, is not just a punishment; it is an existential threat. The player is not just losing a game; they are losing a piece of their soul. And so, they return, driven by a desperate need to reclaim what they have lost, to prove that their investment was not in vain.

Cognitive dissonance, our second psychological culprit, is the mental gymnastics that allows the banned player to justify their return. They know they cheated, and they know that cheating is wrong. But they also believe that they are a good person, a skilled player, and a valuable member of the gaming community. To reconcile these conflicting beliefs, they engage in a series of mental acrobatics. They might downplay the severity of their cheating ("It was just a little aimbot!"), blame the game for being unfair ("Everyone else was doing it!"), or even convince themselves that they were the victim of a miscarriage of justice ("The ban was a mistake!"). By creating a new narrative in which they are the hero, not the villain, they are able to return to the game with a clear conscience, ready to once again grace the virtual world with their presence.

Finally, we have the illusion of control, the belief that we can influence outcomes that are, in reality, beyond our control. The banned player, despite all evidence to the contrary, believes that this time will be different. They will be more careful, more discreet, and more clever. They will outsmart the game developers, the anti-cheat software, and the legions of players who are actively trying to get them banned again. This illusion of control is a powerful motivator, a shot of adrenaline that fuels their return. It is the same psychological quirk that convinces people to play the lottery, to invest in a failing stock, or to try and reason with a cat. It is the triumph of hope over experience, the unwavering belief that, against all odds, we can bend the universe to our will.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The annals of gaming history are littered with the tales of banned players who, like digital boomerangs, just keep coming back. One of the most infamous examples is the case of "Vinny," a professional Counter-Strike player who was banned for cheating in a major tournament. Despite being publicly shamed and ostracized from the professional gaming community, Vinny was repeatedly caught playing the game under various aliases, his signature style of impossibly accurate headshots giving him away every time. His story is a classic example of the sunk cost fallacy in action. Vinny had invested his entire life in becoming a professional gamer, and he was not about to let a little thing like a lifetime ban stand in his way.

Then there is the cautionary tale of "The Griefer of Azeroth," a World of Warcraft player who was notorious for his disruptive and toxic behavior. He would spend hours harassing other players, disrupting in-game events, and generally making a nuisance of himself. He was banned countless times, yet he always returned, each time with a new character and a new reign of terror. His motivation, it seems, was not to win, but to simply cause chaos. He was a digital anarchist, a Loki in the land of elves and dwarves, and his repeated returns were a testament to the power of a certain type of personality to thrive on negative attention.

More recently, the world of Fortnite was rocked by the scandal of a popular streamer who was caught using an aimbot during a live broadcast. The public outcry was immediate and severe, and the streamer was promptly banned from the game. Yet, within weeks, he was back, streaming the game from a new account and a new platform. His justification? He was a content creator, and Fortnite was his content. To stop playing would be to abandon his career, his livelihood, and his legion of fans. His story is a modern-day morality play, a cautionary tale about the dangers of building a career on a foundation of digital sand.

These are just a few of the many stories of banned players who have refused to accept their fate. They are the ghosts in the machine, the digital outlaws who haunt the servers of our favorite games. They are a reminder that, in the world of online gaming, the rules are not always as clear-cut as they seem, and that the human desire for a second chance, or a third, or a fourth, is a powerful and persistent force.

## DISCUSSION

The phenomenon of the returning banned player, while amusing on the surface, has profound implications for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits. It is a microcosm of our larger struggles with addiction, our inability to let go of bad investments, and our desperate need for a sense of belonging. The banned player is not just a cheater; they are a reflection of our own worst impulses, our own irrational desires, and our own unwavering belief in the power of a second chance.

From a consumer behavior perspective, the returning banned player is a fascinating case study in brand loyalty, albeit a twisted and dysfunctional form of it. These are customers who have been explicitly told that their business is no longer wanted, yet they continue to patronize the brand, often at great personal expense. This suggests that the traditional models of customer relationship management are ill-equipped to deal with this type of consumer. A simple "thank you for your business" is not going to cut it when the customer is actively trying to break your product.

Furthermore, the behavior of the returning banned player highlights the importance of community in the world of online gaming. For many players, the game is not just a game; it is a social space, a place where they have formed friendships, rivalries, and a sense of belonging. To be banned from the game is to be exiled from this community, a punishment that can be far more severe than the loss of a few digital items. The desire to reconnect with this community, to reclaim their place in the social hierarchy, is a powerful motivator for the returning banned player.

Finally, the phenomenon of the returning banned player serves as a cautionary tale for game developers. It is a reminder that the rules of the game are not the only rules that matter. The psychological needs and motivations of the players are just as important, if not more so. A game that is perceived as unfair, or a community that is perceived as toxic, is a breeding ground for cheaters and malcontents. By understanding the psychology of the banned player, game developers can create games that are not only more fair and balanced, but also more engaging and rewarding for all players.

## CONCLUSION

In the end, the returning banned player is a tragic figure, a digital Sisyphus forever pushing a boulder of their own making up a virtual hill. They are driven by a potent cocktail of psychological forces, from the sunk cost fallacy to the illusion of control, that compels them to return to the scene of their digital crimes, time and time again. They are a testament to the power of human irrationality, our unwavering belief in second chances, and our remarkable ability to justify our own bad behavior.

But perhaps there is a lesson to be learned from these digital outlaws. Perhaps they are a reminder that we are all, in our own way, returning banned players. We all have our own sunk costs, our own cognitive dissonances, and our own illusions of control. We all have our own virtual worlds that we refuse to let go of, our own digital ghosts that we continue to chase. And so, the next time you encounter a banned player who has returned to the game, do not be too quick to judge. For in their digital struggles, you may just see a reflection of your own.
